title: Watch: Kaine asks whether Maduro's arrest or Khamenei's killing is regime change
author: CBS News
fullText: Senator Kaine. >> Thank you, Mr. Chair. >> Been very patient, Senator Kane. >> Thank you, Mr. Chair. Thanks, Mr. Secretary. Um I I don't like responding to colleagues after they've left the room, but colleagues raised to other committee hearings. I get that. Senator Mullen asserted that the anst on this side of the aisle is because we don't like President Trump. Um he has misstated the concern. I I think I can speak for most of my colleagues who have concerns and say our concern is this. >> Have we learned nothing from 25 years of war in the Middle East? More than 14,000 US troops and contractors killed. More than 61,000 US troops and contractors injured, including one on our committee. Hundreds of thousands of civilian lives lost in Iraq and Afghanistan. 8.2 trillion dollars by estimate is what the US taxpayer has paid for the wars and the necessary VA care for those who've suffered and the debt we incurred to fund the wars. And so, you know, you may criticize us for raising these concerns, but the nation, my Virginiaians, have lived through repeated deployments in wars that produced very little that people can see was worth that degree of sacrifice. To also make some corrections, uh, for the record, the Iran deal was a was a bad deal. Well, both President Trump's secretaries of defense and state suggested he stay in the deal. He ignored their advice to get out of it, that it was going to lead Iran to a nuclear weapon. The first paragraph of the deal, Iran committed that it would never seek to never seek, develop, or acquire nuclear weapons, nuclear weapons. That was a forever promise that could have been enforced until President Trump tore up the deal. There was an assertion that, oh, President Obama took us to war in Syria in 2013. No, President Obama proposed military action in 2013 and Democrats forced him to bring it to the Senate Foreign Relations Committee because we said you don't have the authority to wage war without a vote of Congress. We have upheld this constitutional standard against Democratic presidents and we're going to uphold it pursuant to our oath against a Republican president. So that's what we are worried about. Have we have we learned nothing? We hope that this will not be a war that will lead to so many casualties, but six families are already being informed that their loved ones will never come home. And it may well be more than that. Mr. Colby, um I the national defense strategy as the policy lead at the Pentagon, you play a key role in draftsmanship discussion, but then it goes up the ranks and it gets approved by the secretary and it also gets approved by the president. Correct. Uh certainly I I think this it's fair to say I would say this strategy has been more extensively coordinated not only within the department but across >> but just in terms of approval this does not get published on January 23 without the secretary and the president saying I'm fine with >> I wouldn't get specifically into the specifics coordination obviously it's signed by the secretary of war um it's on his statutory responsibilities but I would also say I I would I would assert that this is the most extensively coordinated um defense. >> So the Secretary of War is not going to issue it unless the president agrees. Now this was January 23, 6 weeks ago. >> I would say we don't do anything and certainly not something as significant as this without being aligned with the president. >> Yeah. So you you've agreed with me and taken 10 seconds of my time to do so. So I mean the national defense strategy, no longer will the department be distracted by interventionism, endless wars, regime change, and nation building. Venezuela and and Iran. I'm not going to ask you whether or not that's interventionism because I'm not going to embarrass you by making you try to say that it's not it's intervention warrants. It's interventionism. >> Second, we hope it's not endless war, but we don't know how in particular Iran's going to go. Third is arresting Maduro and killing the Ayatollah. Is that regime change? Senator, I don't think the uh Venezuela I think the Venezuela operation operation absur is a perfect example of how you know my question may if I may respond earlier I think absolutely we have learned the lessons and I thought secretary expressed that very well >> is the arrest of a foreign leader and the assassination of another is that regime change contrary to what you said six weeks ago in the document >> well the uh what I would say is that both operations have learned the precise lesson that you're >> talking is it regime change you can answer that question it. Do you change your regime when you arrest perception of the issue? I mean, how how do you >> I don't I don't know. I I think it's an interesting debate, but I think in the case of Venezuela, there's >> Let me just say then, okay, you you are uncomfortable having your own words read to you and you're uncomfortable saying that the assassination of a leader and the arrest of a leader, you won't acknowledge that's regime change. I think that's very instructive to those listening. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Uh, thank you very
